narrative: You push through the damp undergrowth and notice tall clumps of broad grass-like leaves with long drooping tassels swaying gently in the breeze...
interesting_fact: Pendulous Sedge can indicate ancient woodland — its presence alongside other indicator species suggests the site has been continuously wooded since the 17th century.

questions.question: Which of these plants is Pendulous Sedge?
Does Pendulous Sedge have any other common names?
options: Hanging Sedge
Goosegrass
Winterweed
Alehoof

questions.question: What is the botanical name of Pendulous Sedge?
options: Carex pendula
Carex sylvatica
Juncus effusus
Phalaris arundinacea

questions.question: Where is Pendulous Sedge typically found?
options: Damp woodland on heavy clay soil, riverbanks and ditches
Dry chalk downland and open grassland in full sun
Rocky coastal cliffs and exposed hillsides
Open moorland and upland bogs
questions.correct: Damp woodland on heavy clay soil, riverbanks and ditches

questions.question: Select all key identifying features of Pendulous Sedge
options: Long broad grass-like leaves up to 20mm wide
Triangular stem in cross section
Long drooping tassels hanging from tall stems
Strong minty smell when crushed
Tiny hooked hairs that cling to clothing
correct: Long broad grass-like leaves up to 20mm wide
Long drooping tassels hanging from tall stems

questions.question: How can you tell a sedge from a grass?
options: Sedges have edges — triangular stems with sharp edges. Grasses have round hollow stems
Sedges always grow near water whereas grasses grow in dry open ground
Sedges have broader leaves than grasses and a stronger smell when crushed
Sedges produce berries whereas grasses produce seeds on open spikes
questions.correct: Sedges have edges — triangular stems with sharp edges. Grasses have round hollow stems
questions.remember: Sedges have edges — feel the stem between your fingers and you can tell immediately

questions.question: What is Pendulous Sedge used for? Select all that apply
options: Ripe seeds stripped, winnowed and toasted — added to bread or ground into flour
Leaves dried and twisted or woven into rope and cordage
Young leaves eaten raw as a salad green
Made into a tea or infusion
correct: Ripe seeds stripped, winnowed and toasted — added to bread or ground into flour
Leaves dried and twisted or woven into rope and cordage

questions.question: What do the toasted seeds of Pendulous Sedge taste like?
options: Mild and nutty when toasted
Sharp and lemony with a bitter aftertaste
Sweet and fruity similar to pineapple
Earthy and vegetal like cooked spinach

questions.question: What are the cautions with Pendulous Sedge?
options: Contains silica which may irritate the liver if already inflamed — only harvest ripe light brown seeds
Contains oxalic acid in large quantities which can cause digestive issues
Avoid during pregnancy as it has historically been used for uterine conditions
Safe in normal quantities but avoid if you have kidney or liver conditions
questions.correct: Contains silica which may irritate the liver if already inflamed — only harvest ripe light brown seeds

questions.question: Which species could Pendulous Sedge be confused with?
options: Other sedges — but only Pendulous Sedge has the distinctive long drooping flower spikes
Hemlock (Conium maculatum) — similar tall hollow stems in damp habitats
Lords and Ladies (Arum maculatum) — similar broad leaves in woodland shade
Great Willowherb (Epilobium hirsutum) — similar height and damp habitat preference
questions.correct: Other sedges — but only Pendulous Sedge has the distinctive long drooping flower spikes

questions.question: When does Pendulous Sedge flower?
options: May to June
August to October
December to February
March to April
questions.remember: The drooping catkins are the flower — sedges never have petals

questions.question: What time of year is Pendulous Sedge most commonly found?
options: Year round
March to October only
May to September only
April to November only

questions.question: Is Pendulous Sedge native to the UK?
options: Yes
No — introduced from Asia in the 18th century as a garden plant
Only in southern England and Wales
Introduced by Roman settlers for use as rope material

questions.question: What does the presence of Pendulous Sedge suggest about a woodland?
options: It can indicate ancient woodland continuously wooded since the 17th century
It suggests the woodland was planted within the last 50 years
It indicates the soil is particularly fertile and recently disturbed
It suggests nearby water sources such as a river or pond
questions.correct: It can indicate ancient woodland continuously wooded since the 17th century

questions.question: What is the life cycle of Pendulous Sedge?
options: Perennial — returns every year
Annual — completes in one year
Biennial — takes two years to complete
Ephemeral — only lasts a few weeks

questions.question: What plant family does Pendulous Sedge belong to?
options: Cyperaceae — the sedge family
Poaceae — the grass family
Juncaceae — the rush family
Typhaceae — the bulrush family

questions.question: What is the origin of the name Carex?
options: From Greek kairo meaning to cut
From Latin carina meaning keel
From Greek karpos meaning fruit
From Latin carere meaning to lack
questions.correct: From Greek kairo meaning to cut

questions.question: What does Pendula mean in the botanical name?
options: Hanging or drooping
Pointed or sharp
Tall and upright
Swaying or flexible

questions.question: What is winnowing and where does the technique come from?
options: Separating seed from husk by tossing in the air so the light husks blow away — used for wheat and barley since ancient times
Grinding seeds into flour using two flat stones — a technique developed in the Stone Age
Soaking seeds in water so the husks float to the surface — used in Asian rice cultivation for centuries
Heating seeds until the husks crack and fall away — developed in medieval European grain processing
questions.correct: Separating seed from husk by tossing in the air so the light husks blow away — used for wheat and barley since ancient times
